claims.text: Alex Hormozi defines resilience as a measure of time: specifically, after a negative event causes a change in behavior, how long it takes a person to return to their baseline. A person who recovers in five minutes has high resilience; a person who takes five years to return to normal has low resilience. The duration of disruption, not the severity of the reaction, is what resilience measures.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

numbers.axis: time to return to behavioral baseline after a setback

claims.text: Alex Hormozi distinguishes resilience from fortitude: resilience is the speed of recovery (time to return to baseline), whereas fortitude governs the magnitude of behavioral disruption at the moment of impact. A person can have a severe initial reaction (low fortitude) but return to baseline quickly (high resilience); these are independent variables.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that high resilience prevents adverse events from having a lasting negative impact on a person's life, because even severe setbacks are neutralized quickly by fast recovery. The duration of behavioral disruption is the primary determinant of total life impact from any given setback, not the intensity of the event itself.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Fast recovery (high resilience) compresses the window during which the setback disrupts behavior; a person who returns to baseline in five minutes loses almost no productive time regardless of how severe the initial reaction was.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that resilience, particularly when combined with tolerance, is the most workable lever for people who do not yet have what they want in life. Of the psychological variables affecting achievement, resilience is the one he most strongly recommends developing.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: person does not yet have what they want
evaluating which psychological variable to prioritize

examples.text: Alex Hormozi illustrates low resilience by pointing to people in everyday life who, after something goes wrong, enter a funk that lasts for months. In contrast, the high-resilience person might react intensely in the moment but takes only a few breaths, steps outside, and returns to normal within five minutes. The contrast shows that resilience is not about having a mild reaction but about how quickly the reaction ends.

practices.text: Observe the people in your life and notice how long it takes each person to return to normal after a setback; those who remain in a funk for months have low resilience and are a data point for calibrating your own resilience baseline.
